trajectory_summary: This is a rebook call on a recycled lead — the prior appointment fell through, the customer called back, and the rep secured a new time in under three minutes. The call is structurally thin: no re-qualification, no bill anchor, no curiosity creation, no acknowledgment of the prior miss. The booking is real but fragile — the customer has a hard 45-minute exit constraint, no co-decision-maker status confirmed, and no emotional re-engagement with the value proposition. The rep's brevity is defensible as a low-friction rebook strategy, but it trades appointment quality for appointment speed.
outcome_dispute: v1 tag is unknown. The call ends with a booked appointment (Monday at 5PM), so the outcome should be tagged 'appointment_booked.' However, the appointment carries meaningful fragility: a hard 45-minute time constraint, no re-qualification of the lead, and no re-engagement of the customer's interest. 'Booked' is accurate; 'qualified-booked' would be an overstatement.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: Five. But I have to be done by 05:45 to go to track practice.
primary_coaching_focus.why: This is a recycled lead with a prior no-show, and the rep closes without a single button_up element — no one-hour block statement, no at-computer anchor, no is-that-fair handshake, and no flagging of the 45-minute hard constraint to the closer. The customer has already ghosted once. The is-that-fair handshake is the schema's explicit show-rate lever, and it was skipped on the call where it matters most. The closer also walks in blind to the time constraint. This is the highest-leverage coaching gap on this call.

key_moments.quote: I'm doing great. How can I help you?
key_moments.stewart_read: Rep answers an inbound callback with a generic customer-service opener rather than re-establishing ION's identity and the purpose of the call. This is an inbound callback context — not cleanly covered by either outbound intro template — but the rep still has a job to do: re-legitimize the call and re-anchor the customer's interest before moving to scheduling. 'How can I help you?' inverts the dynamic, putting the customer in the position of explaining why they called rather than the rep re-establishing context. The rep recovers at 00:10 when the customer prompts them, but the opening costs a beat of legitimacy.

key_moments.quote: It looks like we we had time set up to share a solar design with you. It looks like a a, a week or so ago, that ended up falling through.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the rep narrating CRM context aloud — not the customer reporting a grievance. The customer's actual signal about the prior miss is implicit: they called back despite it, which is the strongest trust indicator on the call. The rep has a service-recovery opportunity here — acknowledge the miss, own it briefly, and use the callback as a trust anchor ('the fact that you called us back means a lot'). Instead, the rep moves directly to rescheduling without any acknowledgment. Strategic read: keep it short, minimize friction, get the rebook. This is co-equal with the reactive read given the recycled-lead context and the schema's allowance for context-justified compression. The coaching gap is not 'this was wrong' — it's 'this was a choice with a quality tradeoff.' A one-sentence acknowledgment of the miss would have cost five seconds and materially strengthened the appointment.

Customer volunteers a specific day and time — Monday at 4PM. This is the clearest cooperative signal on the call. The rep immediately overrides it with a rigid schedule menu ('the times we have available would be...') rather than attempting to accommodate or bridge. The customer's offered time is close to the 5PM slot the rep eventually books — a softer pivot ('We don't have 4 on the dot, but we do have 5 — would that work?') would have preserved the customer's sense of agency. The rigid menu is a minor friction injection where the customer was already moving toward yes.

key_moments.quote: Five. But I have to be done by 05:45 to go to track practice.
key_moments.stewart_read: Customer books but immediately flags a hard 45-minute exit constraint. The rep's response — 'that's fine. No. It should be fine. Yeah. We should be able to make that work' — accepts the constraint without anchoring the appointment structure. The schema's button_up protocol requires a one-hour block statement and a no-driving/at-computer anchor. Neither is delivered. The rep also does not flag the 45-minute constraint to the closer. This is a fragile booking: the closer walks in without knowing they have a hard stop at 45 minutes.
